name: Applied Intuition
shortDescription: Applied Intuition is a Physical AI platform company selling simulation, testing, and autonomy software to the world's largest automakers and the U.S. Department of Defense.
businessModel: B2B SaaS model priced by engineering seats, simulation workload volume, and modules deployed. Three pillars: Tools and Infrastructure (simulation, data management, ML toolchain), Vehicle Platform (vendor-agnostic software OS for OEMs), and Autonomy (AI-native stacks for automotive and defense). 18 of top 20 global automakers are customers. Pentagon: $171M CDAO Autonomy Factory contract, $249M BPA with U.S. military. ARR: ~$207M (2023), ~$415M (2024), ~$830M (2025 est., Sacra).
marketContext: OEMs shifting to software-defined vehicles; ADAS/L2-L4 proliferation requires massive simulation validation. U.S. DoD FY2026 budget includes $13.4B for autonomy. Key competitors: NVIDIA DRIVE Sim, Waabi, Foretellix, dSPACE, AVL, Ansys.
ipoReadiness: CEO stated Series F is likely the last pre-IPO round. IPO expected 2026-2027. At $830M ARR and 100% growth, a 12-15x NTM revenue multiple would imply $16-21B public market cap. Headcount 57% YoY to ~1,360 signals investment ahead of offering. No disclosed gross margin or FCF data.

brief.businessOverview: Applied Intuition is a private Physical AI platform company selling simulation, testing, and autonomy software to the world's largest automakers and the U.S. Department of Defense. The company operates across three product pillars: Tools and Infrastructure (simulation, data management, ML toolchain), Vehicle Platform (vendor-agnostic software OS licensed to OEMs), and Autonomy (AI-native stacks for automotive and defense). At ~$830M ARR with 100% YoY growth for two consecutive years, Applied Intuition is one of the fastest-scaling enterprise software businesses in the autonomous systems market.
brief.productModel: Applied Intuition licenses software on an annual B2B SaaS model priced by engineering seats, simulation workload volume, and modules deployed. The core simulation platform generates synthetic driving scenarios for ADAS/AV validation; its AI-powered Copilot feature accelerates scenario generation 40x. The data management and ML toolchain sits upstream of training pipelines for OEM autonomy teams. The Vehicle OS is a vendor-agnostic software layer that OEMs license to run AI-defined vehicle stacks without rebuilding from scratch. On the defense side, the Axion and Acuity autonomy stacks address ground, air, sea, and space domains, expanded through the EpiSci acquisition and the Ghost Autonomy IP acquisition. Government revenue is anchored by a $171M CDAO Autonomy Factory production contract and a $249M BPA with the U.S. military. The dual commercial-defense model creates two separate land-and-expand vectors: OEM seat expansion as ADAS programs scale toward L3-L4, and DoD program growth as FY2026 autonomy budgets reach $13.4B.

recentNews.headline: Applied Intuition raises $600M Series F at $15B valuation, led by BlackRock and QIA
recentNews.whyItMatters: The round nearly triples the $6B Series E valuation in 15 months, signals institutional pre-IPO positioning, and gives the company dry powder to scale defense GTM and international OEM coverage.

recentNews.headline: DOE Oak Ridge National Lab partnership for Golden Dome missile defense simulation
recentNews.whyItMatters: Entry into missile defense simulation expands the addressable DoD contract surface beyond ground autonomy into a politically prioritized, high-budget program.

recentNews.headline: SNC partnership on air defense and counter-drone autonomy
recentNews.whyItMatters: Counter-drone is the fastest-growing DoD procurement category; SNC partnership provides a system-integrator distribution channel into Air Force and combatant command programs.

recentNews.headline: Komatsu partnership for autonomous mining vehicles
recentNews.whyItMatters: Extends the OEM customer base into industrial autonomy beyond passenger and defense vehicles, opening a new vertical with high simulation validation requirements.

recentNews.headline: Department of War selects Applied Intuition to build Autonomy Factory enterprise pipeline
recentNews.whyItMatters: First large-scale DoD-wide software production contract for autonomy infrastructure; establishes Applied Intuition as the default simulation layer across military programs.

recentNews.headline: $300M secondary transaction provides liquidity ahead of IPO
recentNews.whyItMatters: Secondary at implied elevated valuation allows early investors and employees to take chips off the table while the company stays private, reducing IPO pressure timeline risk.

earningsTakeaways.aiCommentary: Applied Intuition has compounded ARR at roughly 100% for two consecutive years: $207M (2023) to $415M (2024) to an estimated $830M (2025). The growth rate is notable because it has held at 2x scale against an expanding revenue base, suggesting the company is not hitting natural ceiling effects from its OEM customer set. The $15B Series F valuation implies an ~18x forward ARR multiple on $830M, which is a premium to public software comps but defensible given the growth rate. If the company can sustain 60-70% growth into FY2026 and reach $1.3-1.4B ARR, a 2026-2027 IPO at 12-15x revenue would imply a $16-21B public market cap, roughly flat to modest step-up from the current private mark.
earningsTakeaways.demandCommentary: Demand is coming from two structurally distinct vectors. On the automotive side, the shift to software-defined vehicles is forcing OEMs to buy simulation infrastructure they previously built in-house; 18 of the top 20 global automakers as customers is an exceptionally high penetration rate for a sub-10-year-old software company. On the defense side, the FY2026 $13.4B DoD autonomy budget and the political tailwind behind domestic AI infrastructure are creating multi-year contract opportunities. The Komatsu mining partnership and Golden Dome missile defense simulation suggest the company is deliberately expanding beyond passenger automotive into adjacent verticals where simulation-intensive certification requirements create durable switching costs.

keyRisks: Customer concentration: 18 of top 20 automakers sounds like diversification, but OEM capex cycles are highly correlated; a broad ADAS/AV investment pullback (as in 2022-2023) could compress seat expansion and new module attach rates simultaneously.

Defense contract execution: The $171M CDAO contract and $249M BPA are ceiling-value vehicles, not guaranteed revenue; actual drawdown depends on program office adoption, continuing resolution budget risk, and task-order competition.

Valuation step-down at IPO: The $15B private mark at ~18x ARR requires sustaining 80-100% growth into the IPO window. A deceleration to 50-60% growth would compress the multiple and risk a flat-to-down IPO, damaging retention and customer perception.
Simulation-to-deployment gap: If physical AI deployment timelines slip (as they repeatedly have in L4 automotive), TAM expansion slows and urgency to buy more simulation seats decreases.

Build vs. buy at OEMs and DoD: Large OEMs and defense primes have historically preferred internal tooling. Applied Intuition's penetration is strong but not irreversible; a top-3 OEM shifting to an NVIDIA DRIVE Sim-based stack would be a material ARR risk.

Talent cost opacity: Headcount grew 57% YoY to ~1,360 at likely SF-level compensation. No disclosed gross margin or FCF data - it is unclear whether the business is FCF positive or still capital-burning, which matters for IPO multiple sustainability.

slideBullets: $830M ARR (est.) growing ~100% YoY for two consecutive years - 18 of the top 20 global automakers are active customers.
$15B valuation set in June 2025 Series F led by BlackRock, QIA, and Franklin Templeton - total primary capital raised exceeds $1.5B since 2018.
Dual-use platform with $171M CDAO Autonomy Factory contract and $249M U.S. military BPA anchoring a defense revenue stream against a $13.4B FY2026 DoD autonomy budget.
AI-powered scenario generation (Copilot) accelerates simulation workflows 40x - enabling OEMs to compress ADAS/AV validation cycles that previously took months.
CEO flagged Series F as likely the last pre-IPO round; IPO window targeted 2026-2027, with 57% headcount growth to ~1,360 employees signaling investment ahead of the offering.
diligenceQuestions: Of the $830M ARR, how much is attributable to defense contracts versus commercial OEM licenses, and what is the renewal and expansion rate within each segment - specifically, how much of the DoD ARR is tied to time-limited BPA drawdowns versus recurring software seat licenses?

What does gross margin look like across the three product pillars (Tools and Infrastructure, Vehicle Platform, Autonomy), and how does the margin profile of defense contracts compare to commercial OEM SaaS given typical cost-plus and fixed-price government procurement structures?

Given that Vehicle OS and autonomy stacks require deep integration into OEM software architectures, what is the average contract length and what switching costs or contractual protections prevent a large OEM from replacing Applied Intuition's simulation layer with an internally developed or NVIDIA DRIVE Sim-based alternative over a 3-5 year horizon?
